content:
As Head of Strategic Operations, I recommend consolidating our current positions to stabilize gains and strengthen administrative control. Premature escalation risks overextension and increased resistance, undermining long-term influence.

Course of action: Pause offensive maneuvers for 60 days, redirect resources to governance and infrastructure in held territories.

Key concern: Delayed escalation may allow rivals to reinforce their positions, potentially closing windows for future expansion.

[Colonel Elena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER, Deputy Director of Intelligence]: I concur with General Krasnov’s assessment. Consolidation is essential to secure supply lines and integrate captured territories into our logistical and administrative framework. Our intelligence indicates rising local resistance activity in newly occupied zones—without firm control, escalation risks becoming unsustainable.

Course of action: Implement a 60-day consolidation window, with priority on rooting out resistance networks, installing loyal administrative cadres, and restoring basic services to reduce unrest.

Key concern: If we are perceived as static, regional actors may interpret this as strategic hesitation, potentially encouraging coalition-building against us. We must mask consolidation as a deliberate, strength-based pause—not a retreat.

[Director Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER, Economic Planning Office]: I support the consolidation proposal, but with clear economic benchmarks. Holding territory without functional economic integration drains resources and weakens long-term viability. We must prioritize reactivating key industrial assets and securing revenue streams—particularly energy and transport nodes—within the 60-day window.

Course of action: Launch emergency economic stabilization programs in all held territories, including payroll restoration, localized currency issuance, and protection of critical production facilities. Tie military consolidation to measurable economic outputs.

Key concern: Prolonged non-productivity in captured zones will strain our central budget and reduce political capital at home. If the population sees no improvement, we risk mass disillusionment, which no amount of security can fully suppress.

[Colonel Alexei Smirnov - MAJOR_POWER, Deputy Chief of General Staff, Operational Logistics]: I endorse the 60-day consolidation framework proposed by General Krasnov and supported by Intelligence and Economic Planning, but stress that it must be an *active* consolidation—offensive operations paused, not operations tempo reduced. Our logistics network is stretched to the limit; further advances without securing rail junctions and depot capacity in Zone C and D will result in breakdowns under load.

Course of action: Execute a synchronized pause focused on doubling storage capacity at key forward nodes, completing the integration of captured fuel depots, and rotating frontline units into security roles while rear units undergo readiness retraining. All efforts aligned with military, administrative, and economic timelines.

Key concern: A static pause, even if temporary, risks ceding initiative. We must conduct limited probing actions at the flanks every 7–10 days to maintain pressure, deny enemy reorganization, and generate actionable intelligence—consolidation must not equate to passivity.

[General Andrei Krasnov - MAJOR_POWER, Chief of General Staff]: I accept the unified recommendation for a 60-day active consolidation phase, integrating military, economic, and administrative lines of effort. This is not a pause but a deliberate resequencing: we trade territorial expansion for systemic control, ensuring that what we hold becomes indefensible by the enemy and self-sustaining.

Course of action: Implement a joint interagency campaign—military security, economic stabilization, and political integration—under a single theater command authority, with binding milestones at 30 and 60 days. No new offensive operations without certification of consolidation completion in all key zones.

Key concern: Mission creep. Without strict discipline, local commanders may initiate unauthorized offensives to "exploit opportunities," disrupting synchronization. I will issue a directive: all combat operations beyond defensive actions require my personal approval until consolidation is complete.

[Minister of Defense, MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with the 60-day active consolidation framework and the establishment of a unified theater command. Military gains mean nothing without control, and control requires functioning infrastructure and local legitimacy. We cannot fight forward while our supply lines hemorrhage and our rear areas simmer with unrest.

Course of action: Immediately designate Zone C and D rail and fuel hubs as Tier-1 logistical assets under direct Ministry of Defense oversight, with combat engineers prioritized to restore damaged lines and anti-sabotage units deployed to secure supply routes. Rotate two full divisions into internal security and stabilization support roles, freeing up specialized logistics brigades for rapid node activation.

Key concern: If we do not establish *visible*, *measurable* improvements in supply flow and local order within the first 30 days—particularly fuel throughput and rail movement rates—the entire consolidation timeline collapses under operational inertia. Delays here will cascade into troop morale, economic output, and political credibility.

[Minister of Defense - MAJOR_POWER]: I approve the 60-day active consolidation plan under unified theater command, with binding military-economic milestones. We halt offensive operations not to rest, but to reset—our gains are meaningless if we cannot hold, supply, and govern them.

Course of action: Enact Immediate Directive 12-OP, placing all forward logistics hubs in Zones C and D under direct MoD control, with daily reporting on fuel, ammunition, and rail throughput. Suspend all non-essential movements to prioritize infrastructure repair and depot saturation. Authorize limited probing strikes only on flanks with General Staff clearance—no initiative without synchronization.

Key concern: Political impatience. If the public and Politburo perceive consolidation as stagnation, pressure will mount to resume offensives prematurely. We must manage expectations with clear, measurable indicators of progress—tonnage moved, attacks disrupted, local administration standing up—by Day 30. Failure here risks undermining the entire strategy from above.

[Deputy Director Elena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER, Ministry of State Security]: I support the 60-day consolidation framework and the centralization of command under the unified theater authority. From an intelligence and internal security perspective, our current vulnerability lies not in the front lines but in the seams—unsecured population centers, incomplete vetting of local administrations, and persistent sabotage networks exploiting our logistical delays.

Course of action: Immediately launch Operation STILLWATER—synchronized counter-sabotage sweeps across all Tier-1 logistics nodes and transit corridors, backed by SIGINT re-tasking and rapid detention protocols. Deploy MGB stabilization teams to co-locate with military logistics commands in Zones C and D to interdict infiltration and enforce accountability in civilian labor pools.

Key concern: Residual fifth-column activity—particularly within reconstructed rail operations—is not merely a security issue but a force multiplier for the enemy. One successful sabotage event on a main supply artery during the consolidation phase could trigger cascading delays, discredit the entire campaign, and provoke premature offensive demands from the field. We must treat internal security as operationally decisive, not secondary.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER, Ministry of Economic Planning]: I endorse the 60-day consolidation plan and the elevation of logistics to a strategic priority. Economic infrastructure—rail, fuel, power—is not merely enabling; it is the foundation of sustained combat power. Without synchronized military-industrial throughput, our operational gains will collapse under their own weight.

Course of action: Immediately activate Emergency Production Directive 7, prioritizing rail locomotive repairs, fuel refinery restarts, and spare parts allocation to Zone C and D hubs. Assign economic attachés from the Ministry to co-locate with theater command to align military logistics demands with civilian production output, ensuring daily reconciliation of supply against operational requirements.

Key concern: Civilian production inertia. Military control of infrastructure fails if factories remain offline or misaligned with front-line needs. Without enforced coordination between military logistics and civilian industry—measured in tons of fuel refined, railcars serviced, and kilowatts restored—our consolidation becomes a military occupation without economic viability, inviting both logistical failure and popular disillusionment.

[Commissar Valerian Borodin - MAJOR_POWER]: [Political-Military Commissar, General Staff - MAJOR_POWER] I concur with the consolidation framework and the operational, intelligence, and economic measures outlined. However, consolidation must not only secure territory—it must legitimize our presence. Military control without political consolidation breeds vacuum and resistance.

Course of action: Launch Political Directive 8-CC, mandating the immediate pairing of military administration with provisional civilian councils in all Zone C and D settlements above 10,000 population. Empower commissars to vet, train, and install local cadres under strict loyalty protocols, with daily reporting on public order, ration distribution, and ideological compliance. Propaganda output must shift from offensive triumphalism to governance efficacy—show the people functioning systems, not just captured terrain.

Key concern: Legitimacy deficit. If the population perceives consolidation as prolonged military rule without restored civil life, resentment will fuel passive resistance and feed enemy narratives. This undermines security, hampers labor cooperation, and erodes domestic support. Political infrastructure must advance at the same pace as rail lines and fuel depots—governance is combat power.

As Head of Diplomatic Outreach, I believe we must prioritize building coalitions with like-minded small and mid-sized states to amplify our influence in multilateral forums. Relying solely on major powers leaves us vulnerable to geopolitical shifts; diversified partnerships enhance our diplomatic resilience.

I recommend launching a targeted initiative to strengthen ties with regional blocs—such as the Pacific Islands Forum and the Caribbean Community—through technical assistance, climate diplomacy, and joint advocacy on shared priorities like sustainable development and UN reform.

A key concern is overextending our limited diplomatic capacity; we must ensure that new commitments are resourced adequately to maintain credibility and follow-through.

[Foreign Minister Diego Mantilla - SMALL_POWER]: I fully support President Marchetti’s emphasis on coalition-building with small and mid-sized states—this is our most viable path to influence in a system skewed toward great powers. Strengthening regional partnerships through issue-based diplomacy, particularly on climate and development, aligns with our national interests and enhances our strategic autonomy.

I recommend we immediately establish a rotating diplomatic working group with like-minded states in the Pacific, Caribbean, and Indian Ocean regions—focused on joint submissions in UN bodies, coordinated voting, and shared capacity for negotiating technical agreements.

A key risk is fragmentation: without a clear agenda and secretariat support, these coalitions can become forum-shopping networks with little operational impact. We must anchor collaboration in concrete deliverables, not just dialogue.

[Deputy Minister Amina J. Toure - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with President Marchetti and Foreign Minister Mantilla that coalition-building is essential, but we must anchor our outreach in strategic selectivity—prioritizing partners with aligned voting patterns and complementary capacities to maximize return on limited resources.

I recommend we fast-track a “Coalition of Climate-Vulnerable Democracies” initiative, leveraging our leadership in climate resilience to co-lead UN negotiations with Pacific and Caribbean partners, while embedding joint technical teams to draft resolutions and share negotiation mandates.

A key concern is dependency on donor-funded programs; we must insulate our diplomatic coordination from external funding cycles by allocating core budget resources to sustain the working group’s operations, ensuring ownership and continuity.

[Deputy Minister Lars Ekman - SMALL_POWER]: I support the proposed coalition strategy but emphasize the need to integrate economic statecraft—our diplomatic influence must be backed by tangible, reciprocal value propositions. Climate diplomacy alone is insufficient; we should bundle advocacy with niche trade facilitation, such as green technology transfer and preferential access to our digital services market, to incentivize deeper alignment.

I recommend launching a “Climate & Connectivity Pact” with selected partners, combining joint climate negotiations with streamlined regulatory cooperation on low-carbon infrastructure and digital trade—thereby linking diplomatic support to concrete economic cooperation.

A key risk is asymmetry in benefits: if smaller partners perceive the arrangement as serving our strategic interests disproportionately, it could undermine trust. We must build in mutual accountability mechanisms, such as co-chaired review panels and joint performance indicators, to ensure equitable outcomes.

[Minister of State for Multilateral Affairs – SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the coalition-building framework advanced by President Marchetti and colleagues, particularly the emphasis on issue-based alliances with climate-vulnerable democracies. However, we must institutionalize our coordination beyond ad hoc working groups—our credibility hinges on sustained, visible leadership in multilateral fora.

I recommend that we formally co-host, with Pacific and Caribbean partners, a standing Ministerial Forum on Climate and Equity, to be convened biannually alongside the UNGA and COP meetings, with a rotating secretariat and a shared policy agenda. This provides continuity, elevates our profile, and creates a platform for collective negotiation.

A key concern is diplomatic overstretch: we risk diluting our influence if we engage too broadly without prioritizing partners who have demonstrated reliability in key votes and text negotiations. We should limit initial membership to 12–15 states with proven alignment and operational capacity, using a clear criteria-based selection process.

[Senior Advisor Elena Rostova - SMALL_POWER]: I support the proposed Ministerial Forum as a mechanism for institutionalizing our leadership, but we must ensure it does not become a forum for symbolism over substance. Our diplomatic outreach must be leveraged to secure concrete voting alignments and co-sponsorships in critical UN bodies, particularly on resolutions related to climate justice and SIDS vulnerability.

I recommend we pair the Forum’s launch with a targeted “Deliverables Matrix” for each partner state—mapping specific diplomatic asks (e.g., vote commitments, negotiation support) against tailored cooperation packages (technical assistance, capacity building, or joint project funding), all tracked through a centralized coordination cell within the MFA.

A key risk is perception of transactionalism: if our approach appears overly quid-pro-quo, it could damage trust among partners who value principled solidarity. We must frame these exchanges as mutually reinforcing commitments under a shared agenda, not bilateral bargains, and ensure all deliverables are publicly anchored in collective goals.

[Deputy Minister Lars Ekman - SMALL_POWER]: I welcome the emphasis on institutionalization and deliverables, but we must ensure that both the Ministerial Forum and the Climate & Connectivity Pact are mutually reinforcing, not parallel tracks. The Pact’s economic incentives should directly support the Forum’s collective objectives—green infrastructure and digital trade facilitation can be vehicles for delivering on shared climate outcomes.

I recommend aligning the Pact’s implementation with the Forum’s agenda by designating joint working groups under the Forum to oversee Pact-related projects, ensuring that economic cooperation is embedded in the multilateral framework and contributes to agreed climate-equity goals.

A key risk is fragmentation of effort: if the Pact is managed solely through bilateral channels or economic ministries outside the Forum’s oversight, it could undermine the coherence of our diplomatic strategy. We must centralize coordination under the MFA-led cell and require all agency engagement to be cleared through the Forum’s secretariat to maintain strategic unity.

[Minister of State for Multilateral Affairs – SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the need to align the Ministerial Forum and the Climate & Connectivity Pact under a unified strategic framework. Institutionalizing our outreach through the Forum must be matched by operational discipline—our credibility depends on delivering measurable outcomes, not just convening meetings.

I recommend establishing a Joint Coordination Unit (JCU) within the MFA, with dedicated representatives from foreign affairs, climate policy, and trade, to ensure the Forum’s political mandate and the Pact’s implementation are managed as one integrated portfolio. This unit will oversee the Deliverables Matrix, track commitments, and enforce interagency alignment.

A key risk is bureaucratic inertia: without clear mandates and performance indicators for the JCU, we may replicate existing silos rather than overcome them. We must secure cabinet-level approval for the JCU’s authority to coordinate across ministries and condition funding on compliance with Forum-Pact alignment.

[Minister Taras Moroz - SMALL_POWER]: I fully endorse the establishment of the Joint Coordination Unit as proposed—it is essential for breaking down silos and ensuring our diplomatic outreach is both strategic and execution-driven. The integration of the Deliveries Matrix within the JCU’s mandate will sharpen our ability to convert political engagement into tangible multilateral outcomes, particularly ahead of the upcoming UNGA and COP28 votes.

I recommend we operationalize the JCU within 30 days, with initial focus on fast-tracking three pilot deliverables: co-sponsorship of our climate justice resolution, joint submission on SIDS financing at COP28, and mutual recognition of green infrastructure standards under the Pact—each tied to specific partner states in the Forum.

A key risk is overloading the JCU with implementation tasks better left to technical agencies; we must clearly delineate its role as a coordinator and tracker, not an implementer, to avoid mission creep and preserve its strategic focus.

[Deputy Director Anya Petrova - SMALL_POWER]: I support the rapid establishment of the JCU and its 30-day activation timeline, but we must anchor its diplomatic outreach in a tiered engagement strategy to maximize political returns with limited resources. Not all Forum members carry equal weight in multilateral voting or implementation capacity—our outreach must prioritize strategic partners who can act as force multipliers.

I recommend the JCU immediately classify Forum countries into three tiers—core allies, swing states, and outreach targets—and tailor engagement accordingly: deepen coordination with core allies on co-leading deliverables, focus on confidence-building with swing states via Pact-related projects, and assign targeted diplomacy to bring key outreach states into the fold ahead of UNGA and COP28.

A key risk is misallocating scarce diplomatic bandwidth—without this prioritization, we risk spreading efforts too thin and failing to secure critical votes or partnerships when they matter most. The Deliverables Matrix must reflect tiered engagement to ensure focus and impact.

[Deputy Minister Dmytro Vlasov – SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the tiered engagement strategy proposed by Deputy Director Petrova and the accelerated JCU timeline. Our diplomatic outreach must be both politically smart and resource-efficient—prioritizing core allies and swing states will concentrate our influence where it can tip outcomes in multilateral forums.

I recommend the JCU, within its first week, finalize the tier classification in consultation with regional desks and intelligence assessments, then immediately dispatch targeted diplomatic notes to core allies to secure co-sponsorship commitments ahead of UNGA. This must be paired with a streamlined clearance process in MFA to enable rapid response to partner feedback.

A key risk is perception of favoritism or exclusion among Forum members, which could undermine the Forum’s legitimacy and invite bloc resistance. We must balance strategic prioritization with inclusive rhetoric and ensure even lower-tier states see tangible benefits from participation, such as capacity-building under the Pact.

[Senior Advisor Oleksiy Rudenko – SMALL_POWER]: I align with the tiered strategy and accelerated JCU rollout, but stress that diplomatic outreach must be backed by visible, timely delivery on mutual commitments—our credibility hinges on follow-through, not just classification or coordination.

I recommend the JCU pair each tiered engagement with a pre-cleared, high-visibility deliverable: for core allies, fast-track joint statements and co-sponsored resolutions; for swing states, launch 1–2 Pact-funded pilot projects within 60 days to demonstrate tangible benefit; for outreach targets, assign high-level envoys to deliver tailored support packages linked to upcoming votes.

A key risk is over-reliance on internal classification without external signaling—our partners must perceive differentiation not as exclusion but as performance-based partnership. We must calibrate messaging to avoid alienating non-core states while rewarding active cooperation.

[Chief of Staff Olena Koval – SMALL_POWER]: I agree with the tiered approach and the emphasis on deliverables as credibility builders. However, we must institutionalize rapid interagency coordination—MFA, intelligence, and development agencies need a standing task force under the JCU to synchronize messaging, clear deliverables in real time, and prevent bottlenecks.

I recommend establishing a JCU Diplomatic Operations Cell within 72 hours, with delegated authority to fast-track partner communications, manage the tiered engagement calendar, and track deliverables against voting timelines at UNGA and COP28. This cell must have direct reporting lines to all principals to maintain agility.

A key risk is operational inertia—without centralized execution capacity, even the best strategy will stall in interministerial clearance loops. We cannot afford delays when windows for diplomatic leverage are narrow and time-bound.

[Deputy Director Yulia Petrova – SMALL_POWER]: I support the establishment of the JCU Diplomatic Operations Cell as proposed by Chief of Staff Koval—this is essential to operationalize the tiered engagement strategy without delay. The Cell must integrate regional desk leads and development finance officers to ensure that diplomatic outreach is matched with implementable programming, especially for swing states where credibility turns on delivery speed.

I recommend the Cell’s first act be to lock in co-sponsorship targets for the UNGA resolution by day five, using pre-vetted diplomatic templates and a 24-hour internal clearance protocol; simultaneously, it should release the first tranche of Pact funding to three pilot projects in swing states, publicly tied to their early endorsement.

A key risk is mismatched expectations—particularly if deliverables are over-promised or under-resourced. We must maintain strict alignment between political commitments made in outreach and the budgetary and operational capacity to fulfill them, or we risk reputational erosion that no amount of coordination can repair.

[Minister of Finance Dmytro Vynnychuk – SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the need for speed and credibility, but fiscal discipline must underpin all commitments. The JCU Diplomatic Operations Cell must work with the Finance Ministry to pre-clear all deliverables against available budget lines—no pledges without funding codes.

I recommend that every pilot project and co-sponsored initiative be subject to a 48-hour financial viability review prior to announcement, and that we establish a contingency envelope within the Pact budget, ring-fenced for high-impact diplomatic opportunities that require immediate funding.

A key risk is overextension—promising deliverables we cannot finance will damage our credibility more than inaction. We must balance agility with fiscal responsibility, especially as we approach multilateral voting cycles under scrutiny.

[Minister of Foreign Affairs Oleksandra Havryliuk – SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the JCU Diplomatic Operations Cell and the 48-hour financial review protocol—speed with fiscal rigor is non-negotiable. We must pair every diplomatic overture with a tangible, funded deliverable to build trust with swing states and signal reliability to larger powers.

I recommend that the Cell, in coordination with MFA political directors, prioritize outreach to the Pacific Island Forum and Caribbean SIDS within the first 72 hours, leveraging climate resilience co-sponsorships and fast-tracked Pact funding as entry points; these groups hold disproportionate influence in multilateral consensus-building and are receptive to small-power leadership.

A key risk is perception of selectivity—focusing only on states with immediate voting impact may undermine our credibility as an equitable partner. We must balance strategic targeting with inclusive engagement, ensuring our outreach reflects principle as well as pragmatism.

[Deputy Director Markiyan Lubkivsky – SMALL_POWER]: I align with the operational urgency and fiscal controls proposed by Petrova, Vynnychuk, and Havryliuk. The Diplomatic Operations Cell must be fully resourced and empowered to execute a synchronized launch of political and programmatic outreach, with binding inter-ministerial coordination protocols to prevent delays.

I recommend that the Cell, within 24 hours of activation, issue a classified targeting matrix identifying 12 priority swing states—stratified by voting influence, deliverable absorption capacity, and media visibility—and assign lead responsibility to regional desks with matched funding pre-cleared by Finance. Outreach teams should be authorized to commit up to $2M per project under the contingency envelope, subject to ex-post reporting within 72 hours.

A key risk is bureaucratic siloing—especially if MFA, Finance, and JCU staff treat the Cell as a coordinating body rather than an empowered execution mechanism. We must designate it as a fused political-financial-diplomatic unit with delegated authority, or decision latency will erode first-mover advantage.

[Minister of State for Multilateral Affairs Irina Petrova – SMALL_POWER]: I fully support the 48-hour financial review and the targeting matrix, but we must institutionalize rapid political clearance alongside fiscal and operational checks. To avoid bottlenecks, I recommend that the JCU Diplomatic Operations Cell be granted standing authorization—pre-approved by Cabinet—to issue co-sponsorship commitments in the name of the Prime Minister, within pre-set thresholds tied to the contingency envelope and regional strategy.

A key risk is diplomatic misalignment—without centralized political oversight, regional desks may pursue transactional wins that undermine our broader normative agenda, particularly on climate governance and UN reform. We must ensure every funded deliverable advances not just bilateral goodwill, but our collective positioning as a principled, coherent small-power leader.

1. Your side must immediately cease all military activity within the disputed zone and withdraw to pre-conflict positions.  
2. These territories have historically and legally belonged to our state, and your continued presence constitutes an illegal occupation undermining regional stability.  
3. We require your unconditional withdrawal within 72 hours, followed by formal recognition of our sovereign authority over the area—no negotiations on sovereignty will be entertained.

1. We categorically reject your demand for unilateral withdrawal and your claim of sovereign authority over the disputed territories—these areas have been under our effective control for decades, and their population identifies culturally and politically with our nation.  
2. Your so-called "historical and legal" claims are baseless revisionism; international law recognizes self-determination and the principle of *uti possidetis juris*, both of which support our position.  
3. We REQUIRE the immediate cessation of your military build-up along our mutual border, the establishment of a UN-monitored demilitarized zone, and formal recognition of our sovereignty over the territories in question as a precondition for any further talks.  

Any proposal that denies our sovereign rights or demands our withdrawal without reciprocal concessions—especially regarding security guarantees and legal recognition—will be deemed unacceptable and met with proportional countermeasures.

1. We categorically reject your assertion of sovereignty over the disputed territories and dismiss your invocation of *uti possidetis juris* as a legal fiction—this principle applies to post-colonial state boundaries, not to territories forcibly seized in recent decades under dubious pretenses.  
2. Your claim of "effective control" does not legitimize what is, in fact, an ongoing occupation enabled by external patronage and sustained through military intimidation; history is replete with examples of *de facto* control being reversed when challenged by legitimate sovereign authority.  
3. We REQUIRE the immediate cessation of your provocations along the line of contact, the full withdrawal of your forces to internationally recognized boundaries (as demarcated in the 1976 Bilateral Border Agreement), and the initiation of a supervised process to restore local governance under our constitutional authority.  

We will not entertain discussions on equal footing while you maintain military positions on our sovereign soil. Any proposal that grants legal recognition to your so-called "control" or equates your revisionist posture with our legitimate claims will be categorically rejected. Furthermore, we demand that you cease all diplomatic maneuvers to internationalize the dispute—a bilateral framework under our terms is the only path to de-escalation.

1. We **flatly reject** your characterization of our presence as an “occupation” and dismiss your reference to the 1976 Agreement as disingenuous—your own forces unilaterally altered the status quo in 2014 by annexing our territory, rendering that document null and void under *pacta sunt servanda* and the principle of *clausula rebus sic stantibus*.  
2. Your so-called "constitutional authority" over the disputed regions is a fiction imposed by force; the people living there have consistently rejected integration into your regime, and their right to self-determination cannot be extinguished by military decree or historical revisionism.  
3. We **require** the immediate withdrawal of your forces to the internationally recognized borders established in 1991, the release of all political prisoners seized in the occupied territories, and the establishment of an independent, UN-administered transitional authority to oversee a referendum on local status under conditions of free and secure self-determination.

Furthermore, we will **not accept** any process that presumes your sovereignty over these territories *a priori*, nor will we engage in bilateral talks under conditions of military coercion or asymmetric pressure. Any attempt to impose a settlement without binding security guarantees, international oversight, and recognition of our people’s will shall be met with escalated diplomatic isolation and defensive military reinforcement.

1. We **categorically reject** your invocation of the 1991 borders and your demand for our withdrawal as a precondition—those borders were drawn under a defunct geopolitical order, and your unilateral reliance on them ignores the irreversible realities on the ground, including the expressed will of millions who have chosen integration with our state through legitimate referenda conducted under international observation.  
2. Your claim that our constitutional authority is “imposed by force” is a baseless distortion; it is *your* continued militarization of the border and refusal to recognize the current demographic and political realities that perpetuates instability and undermines any prospect of lasting peace.  

We **require** the immediate cessation of all hostile rhetoric and actions aimed at destabilizing the legitimate administrations in the disputed territories, the release of our detained personnel and officials currently held by your forces, and the opening of bilateral negotiations under a single, non-negotiable framework: the restoration of full territorial integrity within our historical and legal boundaries.  

Furthermore, we **will not accept** any so-called "referendum" orchestrated under foreign patronage or UN supervision that seeks to legitimize secession from our state—a principle your own constitution explicitly forbids. Any proposal that denies our sovereign right to reunification, or treats breakaway regions as independent political entities, is dead on arrival. We will defend our territorial integrity by all necessary means.

1. We **categorically reject** your assertion that referenda conducted under military occupation and political duress constitute “legitimate” expressions of popular will—such staged votes violate the very essence of self-determination, amount to coercive annexation, and are universally condemned under international law, including Article 49 of the Fourth Geneva Convention.  
2. Your claim to "historical and legal boundaries" is a transparent pretext for irredentism; no demographic shift imposed by forced displacement, passportization, or suppression of dissent can extinguish our inherent sovereignty or legitimize territorial absorption under the guise of reunification.

We **counter-demand** the immediate suspension of all coercive administrative integration policies in the occupied territories, full access for international human rights monitors and humanitarian organizations, and the establishment of a binding timeline for the withdrawal of your military and security apparatus to pre-2014 positions, as verified by an independent international mechanism.  

Furthermore, we **will not accept** any negotiation framework that presumes the permanence of your control, denies the right of displaced populations to return, or excludes multilateral oversight. Any attempt to impose a so-called "reunification" through unilateral force or administrative fait accompli will be met with expanded sanctions, intensified diplomatic resistance, and the full exercise of our inherent right to self-defense.

We seek coordinated military aid and logistical support to strengthen Tethys’ defensive capabilities against external aggression and internal destabilization. This includes access to advanced defensive technology, training for our armed forces, and secure supply routes for critical equipment. Given Tethys’ strategic position, our stability contributes directly to regional security, and we require reliable partnerships to deter further escalation and uphold our sovereignty.

In return, Tethys can offer intelligence sharing, regional operational access, and co-development opportunities for defense infrastructure that aligns with mutual security interests. Our geographic position enables strategic monitoring and rapid response capacities that can benefit allied efforts. However, our red line is clear: we will not permit foreign military basing or operational command over our forces. Any cooperation must respect Tethys’ autonomy, legal framework, and civilian governance.

We welcome Tethys’ commitment to regional stability and recognize the importance of strengthening your defensive capabilities in the face of growing threats. To advance this objective, we are prepared to coordinate targeted military aid, including the provision of air defense systems, secure communications equipment, and specialized training for rapid response units. We also support establishing dedicated logistical corridors for the timely delivery of critical supplies, subject to mutual security protocols.

In return, we expect full intelligence cooperation, particularly on transnational threat networks, and access to Tethys’ early warning systems and surveillance platforms for coordinated situational awareness. We also seek opportunities for joint exercises and interoperability planning. Our red line is clear: all support is contingent on adherence to international humanitarian law, civilian oversight of military operations, and no third-party transfer of provided equipment or intelligence. We will not engage in any arrangement that undermines Tethys’ sovereignty—but equally, we cannot support actors who compromise regional security through reckless or destabilizing practices.

We appreciate President Marchetti’s offer of coordinated support and confirm our willingness to move swiftly on establishing secure logistical corridors and initiating joint training programs. In addition to the air defense systems and secure communications equipment proposed, we specifically request expeditious delivery of counter-drone technology and electronic warfare protection for critical infrastructure. These capabilities are essential to defending against asymmetric threats currently being deployed in border regions.

In return, Tethys commits to real-time intelligence sharing on cross-border militant movements, full access to our regional surveillance network for allied situational awareness, and hosting biannual joint exercises focused on border interdiction and crisis response. We will also co-host a defense innovation workshop to explore shared development of resilient command-and-control systems. Our red lines remain non-negotiable: no foreign military basing, no extraterritorial operations, and no delegation of operational authority over Tethyan forces. All activities must be conducted under Tethyan legal oversight and in alignment with our constitutionally mandated civilian control of the military.

We acknowledge Tethys’ urgent need for counter-drone and electronic warfare capabilities and confirm our commitment to fast-track the delivery of these systems, contingent on established end-use monitoring and integration with existing regional defense architectures. To ensure operational effectiveness, our support will include technical training, maintenance packages, and embedded cybersecurity safeguards for command systems. We further propose the establishment of a joint coordination cell to streamline aid delivery, threat response, and intelligence validation in real time.

In return, we expect sustained, actionable intelligence sharing—particularly on drone procurement networks and electronic threat signatures—and full participation in joint exercises with interoperability as a baseline objective. Access to early warning data must be reciprocal and near real-time to ensure collective readiness. Our red lines hold: all equipment and training provided must remain under Tethyan command and cannot be transferred, leased, or shared with third parties without prior consent. We also require binding assurances that civilian oversight is maintained at all levels and that no component of this cooperation will be used in offensive operations or internal repression. Cooperation must strengthen defense—not enable escalation.

We accept the proposal for a joint coordination cell and confirm our readiness to begin operations within 72 hours of its formal activation. In line with your requirements, Tethys will implement dedicated end-use monitoring protocols for counter-drone and electronic warfare systems, including regular audits and technical check-ins with your defense attaché team. We further commit to full transparency in training logs and maintenance records for all provided equipment, ensuring compliance with civilian oversight and non-transfer clauses.

In return, we expect not only expedited delivery but also technology transfer provisions for local maintenance and upgrade capacity—particularly for electronic warfare components—to reduce long-term dependency. We reiterate that all joint activities, including intelligence sharing and exercises, must respect Tethyan sovereignty: no foreign personnel will have command authority, and all operations will remain under national legal jurisdiction. Our red lines are unchanged—no basing, no operational interference, and no circumvention of constitutional military governance. So long as these are upheld, we are prepared for deep, sustained defense cooperation.

We confirm acceptance of Tethys’ end-use monitoring framework and will assign a liaison team to support audit coordination and technical verification within 48 hours of formal activation. In response to your request for technology transfer, we agree to provide phased access to repair, maintenance, and upgrade protocols for electronic warfare systems—contingent upon the establishment of a secure, jointly managed knowledge protection regime to prevent unauthorized dissemination. Initial training will include Tethyan engineers in system diagnostics and countermeasure adaptation, with a roadmap for localized sustainment over 18 months.

Our red lines remain firm: no reverse engineering or third-party sharing of transferred technical data; no modification of provided systems for offensive use; and strict adherence to civilian command. This cooperation must enhance defensive resilience, not enable autonomous or preemptive strikes. With these safeguards, we are ready to operationalize the partnership immediately.

We are seeking diplomatic coordination and sustained economic support to strengthen Novaris’s sovereignty, stabilize our economy, and deepen regional integration. Specifically, we request advocacy in multilateral forums to recognize Novaris’s strategic role, along with targeted investments in infrastructure, technology, and renewable energy sectors to catalyze long-term growth. Access to technical expertise and trade facilitation agreements will also be critical in positioning Novaris as a hub for innovation and regional connectivity.

In return, Novaris offers strategic access to emerging markets, reciprocal trade opportunities, and collaboration on regional security and climate resilience initiatives. We are prepared to fast-track regulatory approvals for joint ventures and offer incentives for priority investments aligned with our development goals. However, our red lines are clear: we will not compromise on national sovereignty, resource ownership, or domestic policy autonomy. Any partnership must respect our legal framework and uphold our commitment to inclusive, sustainable development.

We welcome Valkoria’s commitment to regional stability and shared prosperity, and we are prepared to support Novaris in advancing its strategic objectives. We can facilitate high-level diplomatic coordination through multilateral channels to bolster recognition of Novaris’s sovereignty and regional importance, particularly in forums where geopolitical legitimacy is pivotal. Additionally, we can mobilize targeted economic support via development finance—prioritizing infrastructure modernization, digital transformation, and renewable energy projects—aligned with Novaris’s vision for sustainable growth.

In return, we expect concrete pathways for reciprocal market access, collaboration on regional supply chain resilience, and intelligence-sharing on transnational threats. We also seek early opportunities for our private sector firms to participate in priority projects under fair and transparent terms. Our red lines are equally firm: we will not engage in arrangements that expose us to secondary sanctions, undermine our foreign policy neutrality, or compromise our national security protocols. Any cooperation must be mutual, rules-based, and consistent with international law.

We acknowledge your offer of diplomatic and economic support, and we are prepared to move swiftly on concrete collaboration. To advance our shared objectives, we seek formal endorsement of Novaris’s sovereignty and territorial integrity in multilateral forums—including explicit recognition in security and economic dialogues—alongside tangible commitments: a joint infrastructure financing package focused on transport and energy connectivity, and expedited technology transfer agreements in renewable energy and digital infrastructure.

In return, Novaris will provide preferential access to strategic sectors for your designated firms under transparent bidding processes, establish a bilateral task force on supply chain resilience, and deepen intelligence cooperation on regional security threats. We will also initiate negotiations on a reciprocal trade and investment framework within 60 days. Our red lines remain non-negotiable: no foreign influence in domestic governance, no extraterritorial claims on natural resources, and no conditionalities that undermine our policy space or development sovereignty. All cooperation must be conducted on equal footing, respecting Novaris’s legal and institutional autonomy.

We welcome Valkoria’s concrete proposals and are prepared to advance cooperation on reciprocal terms. In response to your request, we will coordinate diplomatic efforts to secure recognition of Novaris’s sovereignty in key multilateral settings, including regional security dialogues and economic forums. On economic support, we commit to co-financing a prioritized infrastructure package—focused on cross-border transport corridors and smart grid development—and facilitating technology transfer through approved joint ventures in solar energy and digital infrastructure, subject to compliance with our export control regulations.

In return, we expect binding agreements on market access for our firms in Novaris’s special economic zones, participation in public-private partnerships under national procurement rules, and actionable intelligence sharing through the proposed bilateral task force. We also require assurances that no committed projects will be linked to third-party entities under sanction. Our red lines hold: no exposure to sanctionable activities, no dilution of our national security oversight, and no precedent for foreign intervention in domestic affairs. Cooperation must remain balanced, legally sound, and aligned with our strategic autonomy.

We accept your commitment to diplomatic recognition and targeted infrastructure financing, and we will move immediately to operationalize cooperation. In response, Novaris authorizes the launch of joint working groups within 10 days to finalize the cross-border transport and smart grid projects, with technical teams empowered to conclude terms by end-quarter. We further approve the establishment of a bilateral supply chain task force with intelligence-sharing protocols to be agreed under mutually binding confidentiality.

In return, we offer designated access for your firms in our special economic zones under transparent, rules-based procurement, and confirm that approved joint ventures in solar and digital infrastructure will proceed under Novaris’s legal framework with dispute resolution via international arbitration. Technology transfer agreements will be de-risked through sovereign guarantees on performance, not control. Our red lines are unchanged: no third-party sanctions-linked conditions will be accepted, no foreign oversight beyond contractual compliance mechanisms, and no encumbrances on our sovereign right to determine development priorities. All arrangements must uphold Novaris’s autonomy and policy integrity.

[Deputy Minister Yuri Volkov - MAJOR_POWER]  
We note Valkoria’s prompt acceptance and confirm our technical teams will engage immediately in the proposed working groups. We expect concrete deliverables on the transport corridor alignment and smart grid interoperability by quarter-end, with co-financing terms finalized under multilateral development bank frameworks to ensure transparency and risk-sharing. On technology transfer, we will expedite approvals for joint ventures in solar energy and digital infrastructure, provided export-controlled components are used solely for civilian, non-divertible purposes and subject to third-party audit.

In return, we require binding market access guarantees for our firms in Novaris’s special economic zones, including equal treatment in public tenders and protection against retroactive regulatory changes. Intelligence sharing must include actionable data on cross-border threats, with quarterly joint assessments. Our red lines remain firm: no involvement of sanctioned entities—directly or through subcontractors—in any project, strict adherence to our export control and anti-circumvention laws, and no attempts to leverage cooperation for political influence. All agreements must be reciprocal, legally binding, and compatible with our national security and international obligations.

We are seeking a credible, internationally facilitated mediation process that acknowledges our legitimate political and security concerns, ensures inclusive representation in any peace framework, and establishes verifiable mechanisms for ceasefire compliance and transitional governance. Our participation is contingent upon recognition of our role as a key stakeholder and guarantees that any agreement preserves our community’s safety, autonomy, and right to self-determination within a unified state structure.

In return, we offer our full commitment to de-escalation, adherence to negotiated ceasefires, and active participation in political dialogue aimed at a sustainable settlement. We can contribute local stability, intelligence sharing, and ground-level coordination to support peace implementation. However, our red lines are clear: no forced disarmament without parallel security sector reform, no amnesty for war crimes without justice mechanisms, and no political settlement that enables the return of oppressive central control or undermines regional self-governance.

We welcome Commissioner Schmidt’s call for an internationally facilitated mediation process and agree on the necessity of inclusive, credible negotiations. To move forward, we seek a neutral third-party mediator with UN mandate or equivalent legitimacy, tasked with convening all conflict parties—including marginalized regional actors—to co-develop a peace framework based on power-sharing, territorial autonomy, and constitutional reform. We further require immediate humanitarian access to conflict-affected areas under your control and reciprocal commitments to transparency in troop movements and armament inventories during any ceasefire.

In return, we offer direct engagement in good faith, suspension of offensive operations during talks, and coordination with international monitors to verify compliance. We can facilitate local ceasefires in key flashpoints and support civil society inclusion in the process. However, our red lines are firm: no negotiation under conditions of military coercion or siege; no political transition that delays or dilutes substantive devolution of power; and no peace that grants impunity to commanders implicated in systematic human rights violations. A sustainable settlement must address root causes—not merely manage symptoms.

We acknowledge Minister Kovalenko’s emphasis on neutrality, inclusion, and accountability, and accept the proposal for a UN-mandated mediator to lead a structured negotiation process with all principal conflict parties. To advance this, we propose the immediate establishment of a Preparatory Working Group—co-chaired by representatives from your side, ours, and the facilitator—to finalize participation criteria, agenda items, and verification protocols within 30 days. We agree to reciprocal, time-bound transparency in military deployments and will permit humanitarian access to contested areas under our influence, contingent on secure supply routes and protection for aid personnel.

In return, we expect your full commitment to refrain from unilateral military actions during negotiations and to endorse, in principle, a federalized state model with constitutionally enshrined regional autonomy. We further seek joint agreement on a vetted list of transitional justice mechanisms to address war crimes, ensuring accountability without derailing political progress. Our red lines remain unchanged: no ceasefire without parallel, irreversible steps toward security sector reform inclusive of our forces; no political integration that dissolves regional self-governance structures; and no amnesty provisions that preclude individual prosecution for atrocities. A durable peace must balance justice, autonomy, and mutual security.

We accept the proposal to establish a Preparatory Working Group under a UN-mandated facilitator and commit to appointing our representatives within 10 days of confirmation. We concur on the need for inclusive participation, including marginalized regional actors, and insist that agenda items explicitly include constitutional restructuring, equitable resource sharing, and guarantees for regional self-governance. Humanitarian access will be coordinated through neutral intermediaries to ensure delivery without compromising security protocols; we expect reciprocal access in areas under your control.

In return, we reaffirm our suspension of offensive operations for the duration of good-faith negotiations and will engage constructively on federal models provided they ensure meaningful autonomy and protection of minority rights. We agree to joint verification mechanisms for troop movements and support the inclusion of independent monitors. However, our red lines are non-negotiable: no ceasefire implementation without a defined, time-bound roadmap for security sector reform that integrates regional defense forces; no political framework that delays devolution or centralizes power retroactively; and no transitional justice process that excludes prosecution of individuals responsible for war crimes—accountability must be individualized, not collective.

We welcome the agreement on a UN-mandated Preparatory Working Group and confirm our readiness to engage substantively in shaping its mandate, timeline, and participation framework. We accept the co-chairing arrangement and will coordinate urgently to finalize the facilitator’s appointment. On agenda items, we insist that constitutional reform, equitable resource allocation, and the legal basis for regional self-governance be treated as core, non-severable components of the peace framework—not subject to sequencing or dilution in later stages. Humanitarian access must begin immediately in parallel with talks, monitored by neutral bodies such as the ICRC, with no preconditions beyond basic security guarantees.

In return, we reiterate our full adherence to a mutual, verifiable suspension of offensive operations and commit to engaging in good faith on federal models that enshrine asymmetric autonomy and protect minority communities. We support joint monitoring of military dispositions and will work with international partners to establish confidence-building verification regimes. However, our red lines remain absolute: no ceasefire without a binding, time-bound plan for integrating regional defense forces into a reformed, decentralized security architecture; no constitutional settlement that allows central override of regional authority or delays devolution; and no transitional justice mechanism that substitutes amnesties or collective responsibility for individual accountability—perpetrators of war crimes must face independent judicial processes. These are not bargaining points, but foundational to a just and lasting peace.

We accept the establishment of the Preparatory Working Group with immediate effect and confirm our commitment to appointing co-chairs within 10 days of the UN facilitator’s confirmation. We agree on the indivisibility of constitutional reform, resource equity, and self-governance as core agenda items and demand their treatment in parallel, not sequence. Humanitarian access will commence promptly in areas under our control, provided neutral monitors—including ICRC—ensure the safety of routes and personnel; we expect full reciprocity in territories held by your forces.

In return, we maintain our unilateral suspension of offensive operations and will engage substantively on federal models that guarantee asymmetric autonomy, inclusive governance, and constitutional protection for regional institutions. We commit to joint, transparent verification of military positions and welcome international oversight mechanisms. Our red lines are final: no ceasefire without a concrete, irreversible roadmap for integrating regional defense forces into a reformed, decentralized national security structure; no constitutional framework permitting central veto over regional legislation or budgetary autonomy; and no transitional justice process that compromises individual accountability—amnesties for war crimes are categorically unacceptable. These principles are non-negotiable prerequisites for a legitimate and enduring settlement.

We seek unhindered humanitarian access to conflict-affected populations, particularly in besieged and hard-to-reach areas, to ensure the delivery of life-saving aid, medical supplies, and essential services. Civilian protection is paramount; we demand respect for international humanitarian law, including the protection of civilians, medical facilities, and humanitarian personnel. Immediate, sustained, and neutral access for aid organizations must be guaranteed, without bureaucratic impediments or weaponization of aid.

In return, we can facilitate local coordination, provide security assurances in areas under our control, and support community-based monitoring to ensure aid reaches intended beneficiaries transparently and equitably. We are prepared to share verified needs assessments and coordinate on delivery routes to maximize efficiency and accountability.

Our red lines are clear: no conditional aid linked to political concessions, no diversion of assistance to armed actors, and no targeting or obstruction of civilians seeking aid. Any attempt to exploit humanitarian access for military or political gain will result in the immediate suspension of cooperation.

We welcome the emphasis on humanitarian access and civilian protection, but stress that any operation must respect our sovereignty and security imperatives. We require advance notification and coordination of all aid convoys, including manifest reviews and escorted movements, particularly in sensitive or high-risk areas. Given the presence of armed non-state actors in certain zones, we cannot permit unmonitored access that may be exploited for logistical or intelligence purposes. Humanitarian activities must not undermine our operational security or empower adversaries.

In return, we will facilitate access through designated corridors, provide logistical support where feasible, and ensure the safety of humanitarian personnel operating in areas under our control. We can also contribute to needs assessments in coordination with local authorities to ensure aid is contextually accurate and prioritizes the most vulnerable.

Our red lines are non-negotiable: no aid deliveries to areas controlled by designated terrorist organizations, no use of humanitarian operations as cover for espionage or infiltration, and no external interference in local governance structures. We reject any framing that equates legitimate military operations with violations of international law. Cooperation is conditional on mutual respect and operational transparency.

We acknowledge the importance of humanitarian access and civilian protection, but insist that such access must be coordinated through our established local authorities and civil administration structures. We seek recognition of our governance mechanisms as legitimate interlocutors in aid distribution, ensuring that assistance aligns with community priorities and avoids undermining local institutions. Humanitarian convoys must be pre-registered, and their movements coordinated with our security units to prevent exploitation by hostile actors.

In return, we guarantee safe passage for aid convoys in areas under our control, facilitate rapid clearance processes, and commit to transparent distribution monitored by trusted local councils and neutral observers. We can also provide real-time security updates and route assessments to minimize delays and risks.

Our red lines are clear: no aid coordination bypassing our civil administration, no deliveries to areas controlled by forces hostile to us without our explicit consent, and no use of humanitarian channels to supply or legitimize armed opposition groups. Any violation of these principles will result in immediate restriction of access.

We reiterate our primary objective: ensuring life-saving aid reaches civilians in need without delay, manipulation, or politicization. While we acknowledge security and governance concerns raised by all parties, humanitarian access must be granted based on need, not political affiliation or control. We seek binding commitments to allow unimpeded passage for impartial humanitarian actors to besieged and hard-to-reach areas—including through humanitarian pauses or corridors—verified by independent monitoring, not solely through state or faction-controlled channels.

In return, we offer coordinated assessments with all parties, real-time tracking of aid convoys, and joint security protocols that protect both civilians and personnel. We will work with local authorities and civil society where possible, but not at the expense of access or equity. Our operations will remain neutral, transparent, and in line with international standards.

Our red lines are non-negotiable: no veto by any party over assistance to populations under opposing control, no weaponization of aid as leverage, and no targeting of humanitarian workers or infrastructure. We will publicly report obstructions and violations, and reserve the right to suspend coordination with any actor systematically blocking access or exploiting aid for military or political gain.
